words.shortDefinition: Surface indication in code signaling deeper architectural problems requiring investigation.
words.simpleDefinition: A surface indication in code signaling a deeper architectural problem — such as long methods or large classes — that hinders future development. Smells are cues to investigate and likely refactor the existing logic for better maintainability.
words.longDefinition: A code smell is a surface indication that usually corresponds to a deeper problem in the system. While not necessarily bugs, smells represent structural weaknesses that increase technical debt and hinder maintenance. Identifying smells like feature envy or long parameter lists allows developers to proactively refactor code before it becomes a significant impediment to new feature delivery.
words.connector: Think of code smells like check-engine lights — the car might run fine right now, but ignoring the warning means bigger problems down the road.
words.techContext: Common smells: long methods, large classes, duplicate code, feature envy, and data clumps. Identifying them is the first step to improving a codebase.
words.exampleSentence: The function had a strong code smell — it was 300 lines long and touched six different concerns.
words.etymology: coined by Kent Beck; popularised in Martin Fowler's Refactoring (1999). Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/CodeSmell.html

words.shortDefinition: Implied cost of future rework caused by choosing easy solutions over better long-term designs.
words.simpleDefinition: The implied cost of additional rework caused by choosing an easy solution today over a better long-term approach — effectively borrowing speed from the future. Unmanaged debt results in increasing maintenance effort and slower feature delivery.
words.longDefinition: Technical debt represents the eventual cost of choosing a 'fast and dirty' solution over a more robust architectural approach. Like financial debt, it accumulates 'interest' in the form of increased complexity and slower development velocity. Teams must balance speed to market with the necessity of repaying debt through refactoring to ensure the codebase remains healthy and adaptable to change.
words.connector: Think of technical debt like a high-interest credit card — you get the features now, but if you don't pay it back, the interest will bury you.
words.techContext: Useful for communicating with non-engineers why cleanup work has business value. Teams track debt in backlogs and allocate capacity to repay it.
words.exampleSentence: We took on technical debt to hit the launch date, but we've budgeted two sprints next quarter to repay it.
words.etymology: metaphor coined by Ward Cunningham in 1992; expanded by Martin Fowler into a debt quadrant. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/TechnicalDebt.html

words.shortDefinition: Principle advising against building features or abstractions until they are actually required.
words.simpleDefinition: You Aren't Gonna Need It — an agile design principle advising against building features or adding complexity until they are actually required. It prevents speculative generality and focuses the team's effort on delivering immediate, real-world value.
words.longDefinition: YAGNI is a core principle of Extreme Programming that discourages developers from adding functionality until it is necessary. Speculative engineering often leads to unused abstractions that increase cognitive load and maintenance costs without providing value. By deferring implementation until there is a concrete requirement, developers ensure that they build exactly what is needed based on real-world usage rather than hypothetical future scenarios.
words.connector: Think of YAGNI like packing for a flight — don't carry extra weight 'just in case' if you don't need it right now for the journey.
words.techContext: YAGNI fights gold-plating and over-engineering. It pairs with simple design: build the simplest thing that works, then evolve it when requirements are real.
words.exampleSentence: We removed the plugin architecture we'd built speculatively — YAGNI, and it had been adding complexity for a year unused.
words.etymology: popularised by Ron Jeffries during the Extreme Programming movement (late 1990s). Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/Yagni.html

words.shortDefinition: Principle stating methods should either change state or return data, but never both.
words.simpleDefinition: Design principle stating that functions should either change the system state or return data — but never both. This separation makes code much easier to reason about by eliminating hidden side effects from simple informational queries.
words.longDefinition: Command-Query Separation (CQS) ensures that a method is either a command that performs an action or a query that returns data. Queries must be idempotent and side-effect free, while commands change state but return no result. This clarity allows developers to call queries without worrying about unintended state changes, significantly improving the predictability and testability of complex object-oriented systems.
words.connector: Think of command-query separation like a vending machine — checking the price should never dispense a snack, which requires a separate action.
words.techContext: Violated by patterns like stack.pop() (mutates and returns) and getter-with-side-effects. CQRS at the architecture level applies the same idea across services.
words.exampleSentence: The bug was caused by a CQS violation — the isValid() method was silently caching the result in a database column.
words.etymology: coined by Bertrand Meyer in Object-Oriented Software Construction (1988). Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/CommandQuerySeparation.html

words.shortDefinition: Metric measuring the percentage of source code executed during a test suite run.
words.simpleDefinition: Metric measuring the percentage of source code executed during a test run — identifying which parts of the application remain unverified. While high coverage doesn't guarantee correctness, low coverage consistently highlights areas with high risk.
words.longDefinition: Test coverage identifies which branches and lines of code are exercised by a test suite, helping teams find untested 'dark matter' in their applications. However, high coverage is often a vanity metric that does not guarantee code quality or the absence of bugs. Effective teams use coverage to identify gaps in testing while focusing on the quality and meaningfulness of the assertions themselves.
words.connector: Think of test coverage like a security patrol — it tells you which rooms were checked, but not if the guards were looking for the right thieves.
words.techContext: Use coverage to find gaps, not as a target. Branch coverage (every if/else path) is more meaningful than line coverage. 80% is a common pragmatic threshold.
words.exampleSentence: Our coverage was at 95% but we still had a production bug — the tests weren't asserting the right things.
words.etymology: metric emerged with structured testing in the 1970s; Fowler's critique at martinfowler.com/bliki/TestCoverage.html

codeExample.code:
# Run tests with coverage report
npx jest --coverage

# Output:
# Statements : 94.2%
# Branches   : 78.1%  ← the meaningful one
# Functions  : 96.5%

words.shortDefinition: System deliberately designed to be discarded once the problem domain is better understood.
words.simpleDefinition: System architecture deliberately designed to be discarded once it has fulfilled its initial purpose — such as rapidly scaling a prototype. It prioritizes speed to market and learning over long-term stability or perfect architectural design.
words.longDefinition: Sacrificial architecture acknowledges that the best way to understand a complex system is to build it twice. By intentionally creating a version meant to be replaced, teams can optimize for learning and rapid delivery during early stages. Once the business model or technical requirements are validated, the initial architecture is 'sacrificed' and replaced with a more robust, scalable design informed by the previous version's lessons.
words.connector: Think of sacrificial architecture like scaffolding around a building — it's essential for construction, but you'll tear it down once the real structure is finished.
words.techContext: Justifies shipping a simple solution fast rather than over-engineering for scale you don't yet have. Pairs with YAGNI and evolutionary architecture.
words.exampleSentence: We knew the monolith was sacrificial architecture — it got us to market quickly and taught us the real performance bottlenecks.
words.etymology: coined by Martin Fowler. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/SacrificialArchitecture.html

words.shortDefinition: Group of data items that repeatedly appear together, indicating a missing domain object.
words.simpleDefinition: Code smell occurring when the same set of variables repeatedly appear together in function parameters or class definitions — indicating a missing domain concept. These clumps should be extracted into their own cohesive object or record.
words.longDefinition: A data clump exists when a group of data items (like start date, end date, and timezone) are consistently passed together through various methods. This indicates that a cohesive concept is missing from the domain model. By encapsulating these items into a single class or value object, developers can simplify function signatures and provide a natural home for logic that operates on that specific group of data.
words.connector: Think of data clumps like salt and pepper shakers — they're always together on the table, so maybe they should just share one tray.
words.techContext: A clue that a missing concept exists in your domain. Naming the concept often reveals important business logic that was previously implicit.
words.exampleSentence: We kept passing startDate, endDate, and timezone together everywhere — extracting them into a DateRange object cleaned up a dozen function signatures.
words.etymology: catalogued in Martin Fowler's Refactoring (1999) as one of the canonical code smells. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/DataClump.html

words.shortDefinition: Name communicating why a code element exists and what it does without comments.
words.simpleDefinition: A variable or function name so descriptive that it communicates exactly what the code does — without needing comments or implementation inspection. High-quality naming makes code self-documenting and significantly reduces the cognitive load for readers.
words.longDefinition: Intention-revealing names are the cornerstone of clean code, replacing 'how' something is done with 'what' it represents. A good name explains the purpose, constraints, and behavior of a variable or function, allowing other developers to understand the code's intent without diving into the implementation details. This practice reduces the need for external documentation and ensures that the code remains readable even as the system grows.
words.connector: Think of intention-revealing names like clear road signs — you know exactly where you are headed without having to stop and check a map.
words.techContext: The most impactful code quality improvement is often just better naming. Rename variables, functions, and classes before other refactoring — it clarifies what other changes are needed.
words.exampleSentence: Renaming processData() to applyDiscountRules() instantly made three call sites readable without any other changes.
words.etymology: concept from Kent Beck's Smalltalk Best Practice Patterns (1996); Fowler applies it throughout Refactoring. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/FunctionLength.html

words.shortDefinition: Software principle advocating for leaving code cleaner than when it was first found.
words.simpleDefinition: Software engineering principle stating you should always leave code in a cleaner state than you found it. Whenever you touch a file for a fix or feature, perform small opportunistic refactors — like fixing a typo or renaming a variable.
words.longDefinition: The Boy Scout Rule encourages developers to perform 'opportunistic refactoring' by making small, incremental improvements to any code they touch. Instead of waiting for a massive cleanup phase, teams improve the codebase bit by bit during regular feature work. This practice helps prevent the accumulation of technical debt and ensures that the codebase gradually improves over time rather than decaying into a state of unmanageable complexity.
words.connector: Think of the boy scout rule like picking up one piece of litter on every walk — the park stays clean without ever needing a major renovation.
words.techContext: Fowler calls this opportunistic refactoring. It prevents the 'broken windows' effect where developers assume messy code is acceptable because it's already messy.
words.exampleSentence: The PR included the feature plus three small cleanups nearby — the author was following the boy scout rule.
words.etymology: adapted from the Boy Scouts of America motto by Robert C. Martin; Fowler covers it as Opportunistic Refactoring. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/OpportunisticRefactoring.html

words.shortDefinition: Systematic process of restructuring code to improve design without changing external behavior.
words.simpleDefinition: The systematic process of restructuring existing code to improve its design and readability — without altering its observable behavior. It is a fundamental practice for managing technical debt and keeping long-lived software systems healthy and adaptable.
words.longDefinition: Refactoring involves making small, behavior-preserving transformations to code to improve its internal structure. The goal is to make the code easier to understand and cheaper to modify without adding new features. By running tests after each small change, developers can ensure that the system's external behavior remains identical while the underlying implementation becomes cleaner, more cohesive, and better aligned with current architectural standards and best practices.
words.connector: Think of refactoring like editing a draft of an essay — you're making the ideas clearer and easier to follow, without changing the final message.
words.techContext: Discussed when improving code quality, reducing technical debt, or preparing code for new features.
words.exampleSentence: Before adding the new feature, I spent a day refactoring the authentication module.
words.etymology: coined by Martin Fowler in 1999, combining "re-" (again) + "factoring" (breaking into components).

words.shortDefinition: Principle stating every piece of knowledge must have a single authoritative representation.
words.simpleDefinition: Don't Repeat Yourself — a foundational software principle stating that every piece of knowledge must have a single, authoritative representation within a system. Avoiding duplication reduces technical debt and prevents bugs when logic needs to be updated.
words.longDefinition: The DRY principle aims to reduce repetition of software patterns and domain knowledge. It is not just about avoiding duplicate lines of code, but about ensuring that every piece of logic has a single point of truth. When knowledge is duplicated, changing it requires updating multiple locations, which is error-prone and leads to inconsistencies. DRY promotes maintainability by consolidating logic into reusable components that are easier to manage and update.
words.connector: Think of DRY like having one master key — if you change the lock, you only have to update the key once instead of twenty copies.
words.techContext: Balance DRY against premature abstraction. Some duplication is OK early on — wait until you have three instances before extracting.
words.exampleSentence: We had validation logic copy-pasted into four controllers — refactoring to a shared validator was a classic DRY fix.
words.etymology: coined by Andy Hunt and Dave Thomas in The Pragmatic Programmer (1999).

words.shortDefinition: Five object-oriented design principles focusing on maintainability, flexibility, and loose coupling.
words.simpleDefinition: Five core principles of object-oriented design intended to make software designs more understandable, flexible, and maintainable. They focus on minimizing class coupling, maximizing internal cohesion, and relying on stable abstractions rather than volatile implementations.
words.longDefinition: SOLID is an acronym for five design principles—Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion. Together, these principles guide developers toward creating systems that are easy to maintain and extend over time. By following SOLID, teams can avoid common pitfalls like rigid code that is hard to change, fragile code that breaks in unexpected places, and immobile code that is difficult to reuse in other parts of the system.
words.connector: Think of SOLID like the foundation and framing of a house — follow these rules so you can renovate individual rooms without the whole roof collapsing.
words.techContext: SOLID principles prevent rigid, fragile code. Robert C. Martin compiled them from earlier work by Beck, Dijkstra, and others.
words.exampleSentence: The class violated Single Responsibility — it handled both user authentication and email sending, making it change for two unrelated reasons.
words.etymology: acronym introduced by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob) around 2000; popularized in Agile Software Development (2002).

words.shortDefinition: Unnamed numeric constant embedded in code, obscuring its intent and purpose.
words.simpleDefinition: A numeric literal hardcoded into source code without context or a descriptive name. This practice obscures the intent behind the value and makes the code difficult to maintain when the value needs to change in multiple locations.
words.longDefinition: Magic numbers are numeric literals used in code without a clear explanation of their meaning. They make code difficult to read and maintain because their purpose is not immediately obvious to other developers. By replacing these literals with named constants, developers can provide essential context and ensure that the value can be updated in a single location if the underlying business logic or technical requirements change in the future.
words.connector: Think of magic numbers like unlabeled jars in a kitchen — you have to taste each one just to know what it is.
words.techContext: A minor code smell that degrades readability. Quick fix: define a constant with an intention-revealing name.
words.exampleSentence: The timeout was set to 300000 everywhere — replacing that magic number with FIVE_MINUTES_MS made the code instantly clearer.
words.etymology: programming term from 1970s; 'magic' implies unexplained or mysterious origin.

words.shortDefinition: Code smell where a class accesses another class's data more than its own.
words.simpleDefinition: Code smell where a method in one class repeatedly accesses the data or methods of another class more than its own. It indicates that the logic is misplaced and should likely be moved into the class it's 'envying'.
words.longDefinition: Feature envy occurs when a method seems more interested in a class other than the one it is actually in. This typically manifests as a method repeatedly calling getter methods on another object to perform a calculation. This violation of encapsulation suggests that the logic belongs in the other class. Refactoring this by moving the method improves cohesion and ensures that data and the logic that operates on it stay together.
words.connector: Think of feature envy like a chef constantly running to another person's station — maybe those tools just belong at the first station.
words.techContext: Common symptom: long chains of method calls (order.customer.address.zipCode). Often fixed by Tell Don't Ask.
words.exampleSentence: The billing module had feature envy — it was reaching into the Order object's internals to calculate totals, so we moved that logic into Order.
words.etymology: catalogued by Martin Fowler in Refactoring (1999) as a canonical smell.

words.shortDefinition: Code smell where one logical change requires edits across many different files.
words.simpleDefinition: Code smell where a single logical change forces developers to edit numerous small sections across many different files. It indicates that related functionality is poorly encapsulated and has been scattered throughout the system over time.
words.longDefinition: Shotgun surgery is a code smell that occurs when a single change in requirements forces you to make many small changes to several different classes. This is usually a sign that responsibilities are poorly distributed across the system and that cohesion is low. To fix this, developers should look for ways to consolidate these scattered fragments of logic into a single, well-defined class or module, making the system easier to modify.
words.connector: Think of shotgun surgery like changing a lightbulb and having to rewire the entire house — the task is simple but the impact is messy.
words.techContext: A symptom of low cohesion. Often caused by excessive delegation or lack of abstraction.
words.exampleSentence: Adding a new payment method required shotgun surgery — we had to touch 12 files because the payment logic was scattered everywhere.
words.etymology: coined by Martin Fowler in Refactoring (1999); metaphor: a shotgun blast hits many targets at once.

codeExample.code:
# Shotgun surgery symptoms:
git diff --stat

# controllers/users.ts     | 3 +-
# controllers/orders.ts    | 3 +-
# services/billing.ts      | 3 +-
# services/email.ts        | 3 +-
# models/user.ts           | 3 +-
# ... 7 more files changed for one logical change

words.shortDefinition: Overuse of basic types to represent complex domain concepts instead of specialized objects.
words.simpleDefinition: Overusing built-in types — like strings or integers — to represent complex domain concepts like email addresses or currency. This smell results in logic being scattered throughout the system instead of encapsulated within dedicated, type-safe objects.
words.longDefinition: Primitive obsession involves using basic data types to represent domain concepts that have their own rules and logic. For example, using a string for a phone number instead of a dedicated PhoneNumber class. This leads to duplicate validation logic and makes the code less type-safe. By introducing small 'Value Objects,' developers can encapsulate business rules, ensure data integrity, and make the code more expressive and easier to reason about during maintenance.
words.connector: Think of primitive obsession like using a plain box for every parcel — you lose the labels and specialized handling needed for fragile items.
words.techContext: Common in dynamically-typed languages. TypeScript's type aliases help but don't enforce validation like classes do.
words.exampleSentence: We had primitive obsession — emails were just strings, so invalid emails slipped through until we created an EmailAddress value object.
words.etymology: catalogued by Martin Fowler in Refactoring (1999).

words.shortDefinition: Anti-pattern where a single class centralizes too much data and diverse functionality.
words.simpleDefinition: Architectural anti-pattern where a single massive class centralizes too much data and functionality — violating the Single Responsibility Principle. God objects become dangerous bottlenecks, making the system rigid and making automated testing nearly impossible.
words.longDefinition: A God Object is an object that knows too much or does too much, often becoming a hub for the majority of a system's logic. This anti-pattern leads to high coupling and low cohesion, making the class difficult to understand, maintain, and test. Breaking down a God Object involves identifying distinct responsibilities and extracting them into smaller, more focused classes that follow the Single Responsibility Principle, resulting in a more modular and flexible system.
words.connector: Think of a god object like one employee who does everyone's job — they are exhausted, and the whole company stops if they get sick.
words.techContext: Common in legacy code. Classes named Manager, Utils, or Helper often evolve into god objects.
words.exampleSentence: The UserManager class was a god object — 3,000 lines handling validation, authentication, database access, and email notifications.
words.etymology: term from object-oriented design literature (1990s); implies omniscient, omnipotent class.

words.shortDefinition: Code with tangled, complex control flow that is difficult to trace or maintain.
words.simpleDefinition: Informal term for source code with a complex, tangled control structure — making the program flow extremely difficult to trace or maintain. It often arises from frequent changes and a lack of clear architectural design.
words.longDefinition: Spaghetti code refers to software that lacks a clean structure, resulting in a tangled mess of conditional logic, jumps, and dependencies. Following the execution flow becomes nearly impossible, as the code lacks clear entry and exit points. This often occurs when features are added haphazardly without regard for the original design. Refactoring spaghetti code typically requires breaking large functions into smaller ones and adopting structured programming patterns to clarify the system's logic.
words.connector: Think of spaghetti code like a tangled ball of wool — you know there's one single string, but finding the beginning or end is impossible.
words.techContext: Prevented by structured programming, clear naming, and limiting nesting depth. Refactoring spaghetti code often means extracting functions and removing deep nesting.
words.exampleSentence: The payment processing was spaghetti code — eight nested if statements and four callbacks made it impossible to reason about the flow.
words.etymology: programming slang from 1970s; popularized as structured programming replaced GOTO-heavy code.

words.shortDefinition: Quantitative measure of the number of independent execution paths through code.
words.simpleDefinition: Software metric measuring the number of independent execution paths through a program's source code. High complexity indicates too many conditional branches and loops, which makes the code harder to understand and significantly more difficult to test.
words.longDefinition: Cyclomatic complexity provides a numerical measure of the complexity of a program's decision logic. It is calculated by counting the number of decision points, such as if-statements and loops, within a piece of code. Higher values indicate more complex code that is more prone to bugs and requires more test cases for full coverage. Teams often set thresholds for cyclomatic complexity to trigger refactoring, ensuring that functions remain small, focused, and easily verifiable through automated testing.
words.connector: Think of cyclomatic complexity like a choose-your-own-adventure book — the more branching choices there are, the harder it is to track every ending.
words.techContext: Automated tools flag high-complexity functions. Reduce complexity by extracting methods or simplifying conditionals.
words.exampleSentence: The validateInput function had cyclomatic complexity of 23 — splitting it into smaller validators brought each piece down to 3-5.
words.etymology: defined by Thomas J. McCabe Sr. in 1976 paper 'A Complexity Measure'; cyclomatic from graph theory.

words.shortDefinition: Copying code patterns or technologies without understanding their purpose or underlying principles.
words.simpleDefinition: A ritualistic style of programming where developers copy specific code patterns or technologies without understanding the underlying problem they solve. This leads to bloated systems filled with unnecessary abstractions and 'just-in-case' logic.
words.longDefinition: Cargo cult programming occurs when developers include code or follow patterns they have seen elsewhere without understanding why they are doing so. This often results in redundant code, unnecessary dependencies, and inappropriate use of design patterns. It stems from a lack of understanding of the problem at hand and a reliance on 'rituals' rather than engineering principles. Avoiding this requires a curious mindset where developers seek to understand the 'why' behind every line of code they write.
words.connector: Think of cargo cult programming like wearing a pilot's uniform hoping you'll suddenly know how to fly a plane.
words.techContext: Common with frameworks and design patterns. Always ask 'why' before adopting a pattern — understand the problem it solves.
words.exampleSentence: The codebase had cargo cult programming — every file imported Lodash and wrapped everything in try-catch blocks because that's what the tutorial showed.
words.etymology: term from Steve McConnell's Code Complete (1993); references Melanesian cargo cults.

words.shortDefinition: New software project developed from scratch without legacy constraints or existing code.
words.simpleDefinition: Software project that starts entirely from scratch without any existing code, legacy systems, or historical technical constraints. It offers developers complete freedom to choose the most appropriate technologies and architectural patterns for the problem.
words.longDefinition: A greenfield project represents a fresh start, allowing developers to build a system without the burden of legacy code or outdated technology stacks. This freedom enables the adoption of modern best practices and innovative architectures. However, greenfield projects also lack the established patterns and lessons learned from previous iterations. The challenge lies in making sound architectural decisions from the beginning to ensure the new system remains maintainable as it inevitably evolves into a 'brownfield' project over time.
words.connector: Think of a green field project like building on an empty lot — you don't have to worry about the old plumbing or walls.
words.techContext: Rare in practice. Most work is brownfield. Greenfield projects still face constraints: team skills, deadlines, integration points.
words.exampleSentence: The rewrite was a greenfield project — we chose Rust and event sourcing without legacy database schema constraints.
words.etymology: from urban planning term 'greenfield land' (developed vs undeveloped land); adopted in software in 1990s.

codeExample.code:
# Greenfield project:
mkdir new-service && cd new-service
npm init -y
# Choose any language, framework, architecture

# Brownfield: work within existing monolith constraints

words.shortDefinition: Practice of writing code that anticipates and handles potential errors and failures.
words.simpleDefinition: Practice of writing code that deliberately anticipates and handles unexpected inputs and failures gracefully — assuming that anything that can go wrong eventually will. It uses extensive validation and error checking to ensure system robustness.
words.longDefinition: Defensive programming is a form of defensive design intended to ensure the continuing function of a piece of software under unforeseen circumstances. It involves making code robust against invalid inputs, external system failures, and developer errors. By incorporating assertions, input validation, and comprehensive error handling, developers can prevent minor issues from cascading into system-wide failures. This proactive approach improves software reliability and security, particularly in complex systems where unexpected interactions are common and potentially catastrophic.
words.connector: Think of defensive programming like driving with a seatbelt and checking mirrors — you assume others might make mistakes and you're prepared.
words.techContext: Essential at system boundaries (user input, external APIs). Overdone internally creates clutter — Fowler distinguishes between assertions (internal checks, can be disabled) and validation (external checks, always on).
words.exampleSentence: We practiced defensive programming by validating all API inputs and returning clear error messages instead of crashing on malformed data.
words.etymology: term from 1970s structured programming; formalized in Steve McConnell's Code Complete (1993).

words.shortDefinition: Optimizing code for performance before identifying actual bottlenecks through empirical measurement.
words.simpleDefinition: The act of making code faster or more efficient before there is empirical evidence that optimization is actually necessary. This often results in complex, unreadable code that solves a performance problem which doesn't exist.
words.longDefinition: Premature optimization occurs when developers spend significant effort making code run faster before they have measured whether performance is actually an issue. This often leads to over-complicated designs that are harder to maintain and debug. The best practice is to first write clear, readable code and then use profiling tools to identify the specific parts of the system that are truly slow. Only then should developers apply optimizations, ensuring that their efforts provide real, measurable benefits.
words.connector: Think of premature optimization like buying racing tires for a car before you've even checked if the engine starts.
words.techContext: Balance: don't choose O(n²) when O(n log n) is obvious, but don't hand-optimize assembly until profiling shows the need.
words.exampleSentence: We spent a week optimizing the sorting algorithm, then discovered the real bottleneck was the database query — classic premature optimization.
words.etymology: from Donald Knuth's 1974 paper 'Structured Programming with go to Statements.'

words.shortDefinition: Design principle of dividing a program into sections, each addressing one concern.
words.simpleDefinition: Design principle of dividing a computer program into distinct sections — each addressing a specific, separate concern. It reduces overall system complexity by ensuring that changes in one area do not affect unrelated areas of logic.
words.longDefinition: Separation of concerns is a fundamental design principle for managing complexity in software systems. By breaking a program into distinct modules that each handle a specific responsibility, developers can reduce coupling and increase cohesion. This allows individual components to be developed, tested, and updated independently without affecting the rest of the system. Common examples include separating the user interface from the business logic or the data storage layer, leading to more modular and maintainable architectures.
words.connector: Think of separation of concerns like a restaurant kitchen — the chef cooks while the dishwasher cleans, so they don't get in the way.
words.techContext: Foundational principle. When you see a function handling HTTP, business logic, and database access, that's a separation of concerns violation.
words.exampleSentence: We achieved separation of concerns by splitting the controller (HTTP), service (business logic), and repository (data access) into distinct modules.
words.etymology: coined by Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1974 paper 'On the role of scientific thought.'

words.shortDefinition: Designing modules with minimal dependencies on each other's internal implementation details.
words.simpleDefinition: Design approach where system components have minimal knowledge of and dependency on each other's internal implementation. This allows developers to modify or replace individual modules with little to no impact on the rest of the application.
words.longDefinition: Loose coupling is a design goal where components in a system are independent and interact through well-defined interfaces. When modules are loosely coupled, a change in one does not require changes in others, making the system more flexible and easier to maintain. This is often achieved through dependency injection and the use of abstractions. Loosely coupled systems are easier to test and evolve because individual parts can be replaced or modified without causing a cascade of failures across the system.
words.connector: Think of loose coupling like modular furniture — you can swap the legs or the cushions without having to throw out the entire chair.
words.techContext: The goal of most architecture patterns. Tight coupling makes codebases rigid; loose coupling makes them flexible.
words.exampleSentence: We achieved loose coupling by defining interfaces for all service dependencies, so we could swap implementations without changing callers.
words.etymology: from coupling (interdependence) in systems engineering; 'loose' emphasizes minimal connection strength.
